agent_output: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"application_instances","to":"users.preferences_json","status":"risk","issue":"During Step 4 rolling deployment, some instances read from users.preferences_json while others read from user_preferences. If backfill was incomplete, instances reading from user_preferences will return missing or stale preferences — silent data inconsistency with no detection mechanism."},{"from":"backfill_job","to":"user_preferences","status":"risk","issue":"Backfill has no progress tracking or resumability. If the job crashes at row 500K of 1M, there is no way to know which rows were processed. Restarting from scratch wastes time; skipping assumes completion. Both are dangerous."},{"from":"dual_write_layer","to":"user_preferences","status":"risk","issue":"During Step 2 rolling deployment, instances that haven't yet received the new code write ONLY to users.preferences_json. Preferences written during this window will be missing from user_preferences unless the backfill in Step 3 covers them. Since backfill runs AFTER Step 2 completes, this is handled — but only if Step 2 fully completes before Step 3 starts. No gate enforces this ordering."},{"from":"application_instances","to":"user_preferences","status":"risk","issue":"No feature flag means read-source switchover in Step 4 is not atomic. For the duration of the rolling deploy (minutes to tens of minutes), different instances disagree on the source of truth. A user hitting instance A gets old-source data, instance B gets new-source data. If preferences were updated on old-source after backfill, new-source is stale."}],"contract_compliance":"No contract or validation exists between the backfill output and the read-switch. Step 4 trusts that Step 3 succeeded completely, but nothing enforces or verifies this.","data_flow_integrity":"Compromised during two windows: (1) the Step 2 rolling deploy window where not all instances dual-write yet, and (2) the Step 4 rolling deploy window where instances disagree on read source. Window 1 is recoverable via backfill ordering. Window 2 has no mitigation."},"observability_review":{"logging":{"coverage":"missing","gaps":["No mention of logging when dual-write diverges or fails on one target","No logging around backfill progress (rows processed, rows skipped, errors)","No logging when read-source switches per instance — impossible to correlate issues to which code version an instance is running","No audit trail for which source served a given preference read during the transition period"]},"metrics":{"coverage":"missing","gaps":["No metric tracking backfill progress (rows_processed, rows_remaining, rows_errored)","No metric comparing read results between old and new source (shadow-read validation)","No metric for dual-write latency overhead","No metric for preference read/write error rates broken down by source (old vs new table)","No deployment-phase indicator metric to know which step the migration is in"]},"tracing":{"coverage":"missing","gaps":["No distributed trace context to identify which storage backend served a specific user request","No trace correlation between backfill job operations and subsequent reads"]},"debugging_capability":"Very poor. If a user reports wrong preferences after Step 4, there is no way to determine: (a) whether their data was backfilled, (b) which instance served the request, (c) which source was read, or (d) whether a dual-write partially failed. The 3am on-call engineer would be flying blind."},"resilience_assessment":{"failure_modes":[{"scenario":"Backfill job crashes midway through processing","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Add cursor-based progress tracking. Store last-processed user ID in a persistent checkpoint. On restart, resume from checkpoint. Log progress every N rows."},{"scenario":"Backfill completes but misses rows due to query bug or race condition","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Add a validation step between Step 3 and Step 4: SELECT COUNT(*) comparison plus a sampled row-by-row comparison between sources. Gate Step 4 on validation passing."},{"scenario":"Dual-write fails on new table but succeeds on old table","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Dual-write failures to user_preferences must be logged, alerted on, and ideally retried. If the new table write fails silently, backfill won't fix it (backfill only covers pre-dual-write data)."},{"scenario":"Rolling deployment of Step 4 causes read inconsistency across instances","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Use a feature flag or remote config to switch read source atomically across all instances. Decouple the code deploy from the behavior switch. This is the single highest-risk gap."},{"scenario":"Need to rollback from Step 4 to reading old source","handling":"partial","recommendation":"Rollback is possible by redeploying Step 2 code (dual-write, read-old). BUT any preferences written ONLY to user_preferences during Step 5 would be lost. Ensure Step 5 is not executed until Step 4 is validated in production for a bake period."},{"scenario":"Large table backfill causes database performance degradation","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Backfill should be throttled (batch size + sleep between batches). Monitor database CPU, replication lag, and query latency during backfill. Add a kill switch to pause the job."}],"retry_config":"Not specified for any operation — dual-writes, backfill row processing, and read failover all lack retry configuration.","timeout_config":"Not specified. Backfill job has no timeout or SLA. No statement timeout on backfill queries to prevent long-running locks.","circuit_breakers":"None. If user_preferences table becomes unavailable during dual-write phase, every write takes the failure penalty with no fallback.","graceful_degradation":"None designed. If user_preferences reads fail after Step 4, there is no fallback to users.preferences_json. A simple fallback-read to the old column during the transition period would provide a safety net."},"operational_readiness":{"deployment_ready":false,"rollback_capable":false,"rollback_plan":"No explicit rollback plan exists. Rollback from Step 4 requires redeploying Step 2 code, but there is no documented procedure, no tested rollback path, and no validation that rollback restores correct behavior. Rollback from Step 5 is destructive — writes after Step 5 only go to the new table, so reverting the read source would lose those writes.","monitoring_coverage":"No monitoring coverage for the migration. No dashboards, no alerts on backfill progress, no dual-write error alerts, no read-source inconsistency detection.","on_call_documentation":"None. On-call engineers have no runbook for: identifying which migration step is active, diagnosing preference data mismatches, performing emergency rollback, or pausing/resuming the backfill.","new_infrastructure_required":{"new_env_vars":[],"new_services":[],"new_queues_topics":[],"config_changes":["Feature flag for read-source switching (strongly recommended)","Backfill job checkpoint storage (database row or config store)","Backfill throttle configuration (batch_size, sleep_interval)"],"infra_provisioning":["user_preferences table (Step 1)"]},"database_safety":{"has_migrations":true,"reversible":true,"data_preserving":true,"large_table_impact":true,"details":"Step 1 (ADD TABLE) is safe and non-blocking. Step 6 (DROP COLUMN) is irreversible and must not execute until data is validated and bake period completes. The backfill in Step 3 performs bulk writes against user_preferences while the application is live — risk of increased replication lag, lock contention, and elevated query latency on the users table if read queries scan preferences_json during backfill. No batch-size or throttle controls specified."},"verdict":"NOT READY"},"escalations":[{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"No feature flag for atomic read-source cutover","reason":"Without a feature flag, Step 4's rolling deployment creates a multi-minute window where instances disagree on the source of truth for user preferences. This is the highest-risk gap — it can cause silent data inconsistency that is undetectable without observability instrumentation (which is also missing). A feature flag decouples the code deploy from the behavior switch and enables instant rollback.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"missing_observability","issue":"Zero monitoring, logging, or alerting for any migration phase","reason":"There are no metrics for backfill progress, no alerts for dual-write failures, no logging of which source serves reads, and no dashboards for the migration. If something goes wrong at 3am, on-call has no signals to diagnose the issue. This must be added before Step 2 begins.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"Backfill job lacks resumability and there is no validation gate before read cutover","reason":"The backfill job has no progress tracking — if it fails, you cannot resume. More critically, there is no validation step confirming backfill completeness before Step 4 switches reads. Deploying Step 4 on an incomplete backfill means users get empty or stale preferences with no way to detect it. A COUNT comparison + sampled row validation must gate Step 4.","routed_to":"Coach K"}],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Rolling deployment split-brain risk during Step 4 is a real and well-understood problem","Missing backfill validation gate is a clear operational gap","Lack of observability makes the migration un-debuggable"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact blast radius of database performance impact during backfill depends on table size and hardware, which are unspecified","Whether the dual-write ordering between Step 2 completion and Step 3 start is enforced by CI/CD pipeline"],"assumptions":["Deployment pipeline does not have built-in gates between steps — steps are manually triggered","The users table is large enough that backfill is non-trivial (thousands+ rows)","No existing feature flag infrastructure is in place","Application uses a standard ORM or query layer without built-in dual-read fallback"]}}
